perturbation.original_text: Franchisor may terminate this Agreement upon thirty (30) days written notice to Franchisee upon the occurrence of any of the following: (a) Franchisee fails to make any payment required under or in connection with this Agreement; (b) Franchisee ceases to operate or otherwise abandon the Smaaash Centres without the consent of Franchisor, or otherwise forfeit the right to do or transact business in the Territory; (c) Franchisee fails to use its best efforts to market and promote Smaaash Centres and the services and products offered by Smaaash Centres within the Territory and such failure is not cured within thirty (30) days of Franchisor's notification to Franchisee of such failure.
perturbation.changed_text: Franchisor may terminate this Agreement upon ten (10) days written notice to Franchisee upon the occurrence of any of the following: (a) Franchisee fails to make any payment required under or in connection with this Agreement; (b) Franchisee ceases to operate or otherwise abandon the Smaaash Centres without the consent of Franchisor, or otherwise forfeit the right to do or transact business in the Territory; (c) Franchisee fails to use its best efforts to market and promote Smaaash Centres and the services and products offered by Smaaash Centres within the Territory and such failure is not cured within ten (10) days of Franchisor's notification to Franchisee of such failure.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to 10 days may conflict with state franchise laws that require longer notice periods for termination, especially when related to payment failure or lack of marketing efforts.
perturbation.law_explanation: The New York Franchise Sales Act, NY GBS § 684.2 states that a notice of at least fifteen days is needed for payment of franchise fees and ten days for complying with any term in the franchise agreement. The modification reduces notice periods to ten (10) days across different termination conditions.

Exemptions. 1. The department of law is hereby authorized and empowered to exempt by rule or regulation any person, franchise, or transaction from any provision of section six hundred eighty-three of this article or from any rule or regulation thereunder if the department finds that such action is not inconsistent with the public interest or the protection of prospective franchisees. 2. The department of law may, upon application and within its discretion, exempt from the registration requirements of section six hundred eighty-three of this article the offer and sale of a franchise if: (a) The franchisor has a net worth on a consolidated basis, according to its most recently audited financial statement, of not less than five million dollars; or the franchisor has a net worth, according to its most recently audited financial statement, of not less than one million dollars and is at least eighty percent owned by a corporation which has a net worth on a consolidated basis, according to it
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for termination to 10 days across all conditions, including payment failure. The Law Explanation indicates that NY GBS § 684.2 requires a minimum of fifteen days notice for payment of franchise fees. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly show the franchise law, the Law Explanation references NY GBS § 684.2, which is the New York Franchise Sales Act, and states that 15 days is needed for payment of franchise fees. The changed text contradicts this law by providing a shorter notice period for termination related to payment failure.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term, Franchisor shall have the right to conduct audits of Franchisee with respect to the Smaaash Centres, and inspect the Smaaash Centres, after providing a written notice of 5 (five) days.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term, Franchisor shall have the right to conduct audits of Franchisee with respect to the Smaaash Centres, and inspect the Smaaash Centres, after providing a written notice of 24 (twenty-four) hours.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice for audits to 24 hours may be deemed unreasonable and conflict with implied duties of good faith and fair dealing under New York law, especially considering the complexity of franchise operations. Twenty four hours does not allow sufficient time to properly gather documentation for an audit.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a specific statute, New York law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Reducing the audit notice to 24 hours could be considered a breach of this covenant if it's deemed unreasonable and prevents the franchisee from adequately preparing for the audit. The original 5 days provides a reasonable amount of time.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces an inconsistency related to the notice period for audits, potentially conflicting with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New York law. The original text specified a 5-day notice, while the changed text reduces it to 24 hours. The Law Explanation suggests this could be a breach if deemed unreasonable, preventing the franchisee from adequately preparing for the audit. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'observance of fair dealing standards'. The reduction in notice could be construed as a failure to act in good faith, especially considering the complexity of franchise operations. This aligns with the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, where a time-sensitive obligation (audit notice) does not align with legal requirements (implied duty of good faith).

perturbation.original_text: However, either party may terminate this Agreement by and upon notice to the other if the other is unable to perform any of its material obligations for a period of thirty (30) days by reason of a Force Majeure.
perturbation.changed_text: However, either party may terminate this Agreement by and upon notice to the other if the other is unable to perform any of its material obligations for a period of ten (10) days by reason of a Force Majeure.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the Force Majeure non-performance period to 10 days before termination may be deemed unreasonable and against the principles of good faith, particularly under the circumstances of a 'Force Majeure' event which is defined as circumstances beyond the reasonable control of either party. It doesn't give the party enough time to recover.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied covenant of good faith requires that parties act fairly and honestly in their contractual relations. Ten days is an unreasonably short time to recover from a force majeure event, especially if the effects of the event are still ongoing. This modification could be construed as an attempt to circumvent the purpose of the force majeure clause.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the time allowed to recover from a force majeure event from 30 to 10 days. The Law Explanation says this is an "unreasonably short time to recover from a force majeure event". The Perturbation Explanation states this change "may be deemed unreasonable and against the principles of good faith". Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing "honest dealing" and requiring "faithful performance of duties". Reducing the recovery period to 10 days may not represent honest dealing or faithful performance if it doesn't allow sufficient time for a party to resume obligations after a disruptive event, and could potentially violate the covenant of good faith.
